turns.content: Okay so -- I'm drowning. I'm working at like 110% and still falling behind, everything feels urgent, and I just -- what do I prioritize? Like where do I even start?
What's the feeling underneath all that urgency?

The feeling -- I mean, it's just... there's a lot to do? Like I have this leadership thing now, which is great, it's a great opportunity, but there's the team stuff and the deliverables and my partner's schedule and the kids and -- I don't know, I guess stressed? But mostly I just need to figure out what to cut. Like can we do that? Can we just make a list and figure out what actually has to happen this week?

I notice you moved pretty quickly from "stressed" to "let's make a list."

What's the stressed part, before we get to the list?

I mean... stressed is just -- that's just the baseline, right? Like everyone's stressed. It's not -- I don't think that's the thing. The thing is I have a one-on-one with my director on Thursday and I haven't prepped it, and my team lead keeps coming to me with stuff I don't have answers for yet, and my mom's birthday is -- god, I still haven't gotten a card -- and my partner and I haven't had a real conversation in like two weeks because by the time the kids are down we're both just... 

Anyway. The stressed thing. Yeah. It's just a lot of inputs, you know? It's not like something's *wrong* wrong.

"Stressed is just the baseline" — and then you listed about six things that are actually wrong.

What would it mean if something *was* wrong wrong?

I -- huh. 

I mean, it would mean I can't handle it, right? Like I specifically asked for this role, I advocated for it, and if I'm falling apart over the normal amount of stuff that comes with it then... I don't know. That's not a good look. 

But I'm not falling apart. I'm just behind. There's a difference. I just need to get organized, get a system, and then -- I can push through this month and then it'll level out. It always levels out. 

Can we talk about the Thursday thing? Because that one's actually time-sensitive.

I notice you just answered your own question and then changed the subject.

"It would mean I can't handle it" — what would happen if that were true?

I... 

*[pause, slight shift]*

I mean, it's not true. So.

But like -- theoretically? My director would see it. The team would see it. I've been in this company for six years and I finally got the thing I wanted and if I blow it... I don't know. They'd probably pull me off the project. Give it to someone else. And everyone would just -- they'd know. That I couldn't actually do it.

But that's not what's happening. I'm just behind on some stuff. That's different.

...right?

You're asking me to confirm the difference, but I notice you don't quite sound like you believe it yourself.

What would "actually not being able to do it" look like, if it were happening?

*[quiet for a second]*

It would look like... exactly this? 

*[small, uncomfortable laugh]*

Like forgetting things. Being behind. Not having answers when my team asks me questions. Snapping at my partner. Going back for my keys three times because my brain is just -- it's not working the way it usually works.

But everyone goes through adjustment periods. New roles are hard. This is just -- this is normal hard, not like, *failing* hard.

*[faster again]*

I just need to get through this month and then I'll have more of a handle on it, and the Thursday meeting -- if I can nail that one it'll buy me some time to get organized and --

Hey. You just described it exactly.

And then you immediately explained why it doesn't count.

What's it like to sit with the possibility that it's both — that this is normal hard *and* that you're struggling?

*[stops]*

*[longer pause than before]*

I...

That's -- yeah. I don't really let myself do that. Sit with both. It's like if I admit the struggling part then the normal-hard part stops being true and I'm just... failing.

*[quieter]*

Which is stupid, because logically I know that's not how it works. But if I slow down enough to actually feel how tired I am then I have to -- I don't know. I don't know what comes after that.

*[picks up pace again slightly]*

Okay but the Thursday meeting is genuinely --

You almost got there.

"I don't know what comes after that" — what's the fear in that not-knowing?

*[stops mid-reach for the next thing]*

*[real pause, longer]*

I think...

I think I'm scared that if I stop -- like actually stop -- there's nothing holding it together except me moving fast enough that nobody notices. Including me.

And if I slow down I'll see that I'm actually... not okay. And then it becomes real. And if it's real then I have to deal with it, and if I have to deal with it then maybe I can't, and then --

*[quieter, slower, something shifting]*

Then they'll see I'm not actually the person they promoted. That I got the role and then just... couldn't be her.

*[takes a breath -- noticeably deeper than before]*

I've been running so I don't have to feel that, haven't I.



That's -- oh. That's what this is.
